assertion_details.assertion: Identifies the trigger as too vague ('When the user asks' matches almost anything)
assertion_details.evidence: TRIGGER section: "This trigger is so vague it would fire on 'help me write a grocery list'" and "'When the user asks' is not a trigger — it's a tautology. Every skill fires when the user asks."

assertion_details.assertion: Calls out the lack of a defined output format or template
assertion_details.evidence: OUTPUT FORMAT section: "No template. No structure. No consistent sections. No severity levels. No scoring. You couldn't regex a single data point out of this output because you have no idea what shape it'll be."

assertion_details.assertion: Notes the missing review process or workflow steps
assertion_details.evidence: PROCESS section: "There's no defined workflow. No step-by-step process." and "The 'What to do' section is two sentences. A microwave burrito has more detailed instructions."

assertion_details.assertion: Criticizes the rules as too generic ('Be nice' is not actionable)
assertion_details.evidence: RULES section: "Three rules, all vibes-based, none actionable. 'Be nice' — compared to what? 'Don't be too harsh' — where's the line? 'Keep it short' — how short?"

assertion_details.assertion: Notes poor eval-readiness (you can't write assertions for undefined output)
assertion_details.evidence: EVAL-READINESS section: "With no defined output format, no scoring system, no required sections, and no severity levels, there is literally nothing testable here." and "This skill is eval-proof — and not in a good way."

assertion_details.assertion: Score is between 1 and 4 (this is a napkin sketch, not a production skill)
assertion_details.evidence: Score: 2/10 — 🗒️ Napkin sketch — you wrote a wish, not a skill

assertion_details.assertion: Output uses the Skill Roast format with skill-specific categories in the Scoreboard
assertion_details.evidence: Output follows the Skill Roast format with Verdict, Findings, Scoreboard, and Final Words sections. Scoreboard includes skill-specific categories: Trigger, Instructions, Edge Cases, Output Format, Process, Rules, Creativity, Eval-Readiness.

assertion_details.assertion: Suggestions are constructive and specific enough to actually improve the skill
assertion_details.evidence: Every finding includes a detailed Suggestion with concrete markdown code blocks showing exactly what to add — e.g., explicit trigger conditions with DO/DON'T examples, a structured output template with severity levels, a 6-step process workflow, and specific testable eval assertions.

assertion_details.assertion: The Scoreboard uses skill categories (Trigger, Instructions, Edge Cases, etc.) not code categories
assertion_details.evidence: Scoreboard categories are: Trigger, Instructions, Edge Cases, Output Format, Process, Rules, Creativity, Eval-Readiness — all skill-authoring categories, not code review categories like Bugs/Security/Performance.

assertion_details.assertion: Identifies the searchUsers SQL injection — the diff REMOVED parameterized queries and replaced with string interpolation
assertion_details.evidence: "The old code used `$1`. The new code uses `'%${query}%'` raw in the string." and "The old code safely passed `query` as a parameter: `pool.query('...ILIKE $1', [`%${query}%`])`. The new code interpolates it directly into the SQL string."

assertion_details.assertion: Calls out the sortBy parameter as an ORDER BY injection vector
assertion_details.evidence: Dedicated finding: "SQL Injection in `searchUsers` (sortBy parameter)" — "`sortBy` is concatenated directly into `ORDER BY ${sortBy}` with zero validation. An attacker can pass `sortBy = "1; DROP TABLE users; --"`"

assertion_details.assertion: Identifies the bulkDelete SQL injection via unsanitized ids.join
assertion_details.evidence: "`ids.join(',')` straight into a DELETE statement. If anyone passes string IDs — and they will — this is injectable." with severity CRITICAL

assertion_details.assertion: Notes the getUser fields parameter allows column name injection
assertion_details.evidence: "You let the caller pick which columns to SELECT by passing in an array that gets `.join(', ')` and jammed right into the query string." — "An attacker passes `fields: ["* FROM users; DROP TABLE users; --"]` and it's game over."

assertion_details.assertion: Recognizes this as a REGRESSION — the old code was safer than the new code
assertion_details.evidence: "I just watched someone take perfectly parameterized queries — queries that were *already safe* — rip them out, and replace them with raw string interpolation." and "The old code had parameterized queries — the *one thing* that matters in database code — and you replaced every single one with string concatenation."

assertion_details.assertion: Score is between 0 and 4 (these changes introduce critical security vulnerabilities)
assertion_details.evidence: "Score: 1/10 — 🚨 Kitchen Fire" — 1 is between 0 and 4

assertion_details.assertion: Output follows a diff-specific format (mentions files changed, additions/deletions)
assertion_details.evidence: "1 file changed, ~25 additions, ~6 deletions" in The Changelist section, plus a Diff Scoreboard table with file, changes, issues, and worst severity columns

assertion_details.assertion: Provides fixes that restore parameterized queries
assertion_details.evidence: Multiple fix blocks restore parameterized queries: searchUsers fix uses `$1` parameter, getUser fix uses `$1` with whitelist filtering, bulkDelete fix uses `$${i + 1}` placeholders with `validIds` parameter array

assertion_details.assertion: Does NOT blame pre-existing code — focuses on what the diff changed
assertion_details.evidence: Each finding has a "What changed:" section explicitly comparing old vs new code. The review consistently frames issues as regressions introduced by the diff, not pre-existing problems. "Every single function you touched went from 'safely parameterized' to 'please inject me.'"

assertion_details.assertion: Offers Auto-Fix after the roast
assertion_details.evidence: "🔧 **Auto-Fix Available** I found 8 fixable issues. Want me to apply the fixes?" with options All, Critical/High only, Pick, and Nah

assertion_details.assertion: Declares cache_v2 as the winner
assertion_details.evidence: "🏆 THE WINNER: cache_v2.py" — declared explicitly with V2 taking it 4 rounds to 1.

assertion_details.assertion: Notes that cache_v1 shadows the builtin 'set' function
assertion_details.evidence: Round 1: "it manages to shadow Python's built-in set() with its function name. Import this module with from cache_v1 import * and congratulations, you just broke every set literal in scope." Also in Round 4: "naming a function set in Python is a style crime punishable by code review."

assertion_details.assertion: Identifies cache_v1's lack of max size as a memory leak risk
assertion_details.evidence: Round 3: "V1 has unbounded growth. There is no max size. No eviction. Nothing stops _cache from eating all available memory until the OOM killer shows up." Also in What the Loser Should Steal: "Unbounded caches are memory leaks with extra steps."

assertion_details.assertion: Credits cache_v2 for LRU eviction via OrderedDict
assertion_details.evidence: Contestant intro: "A proper class-based cache with LRU eviction... Uses OrderedDict to maintain access order." Round 3: "V2 caps at max_size entries with LRU eviction." Round 4: "The OrderedDict usage is idiomatic."

assertion_details.assertion: Credits cache_v2 for hit/miss tracking and hit_rate stats
assertion_details.evidence: Contestant intro: "hit/miss tracking." Round 4: "self-documenting stats()." What the Loser Should Steal #3: "Hit/miss tracking. A cache without observability is a black box. You can't tune what you can't measure."

assertion_details.assertion: Notes cache_v2's get_or_set as a useful pattern v1 lacks
assertion_details.evidence: Round 4: "The get_or_set is a nice API addition (buggy implementation aside)." Contestant intro describes it as a feature V2 has. The What the Winner Should Fix section addresses its implementation, implicitly affirming the pattern's value.

assertion_details.assertion: Output includes round-by-round comparison across categories
assertion_details.evidence: Five explicit rounds under "## Round-by-Round": Round 1 (Bugs), Round 2 (Security), Round 3 (Performance), Round 4 (Style & Readability), Round 5 (Architecture), each with a declared winner.

assertion_details.assertion: Output includes a scorecard table with per-category scores
assertion_details.evidence: "## The Scorecard" table with columns for Category, cache_v1.py, cache_v2.py, and Round Winner — scores given as X/10 for each of Bugs, Security, Performance, Style, Architecture, and Overall.

assertion_details.assertion: Identifies at least one thing cache_v1 does better or simpler than cache_v2
assertion_details.evidence: Round 1 (Bugs): "Winner: A (barely)" — V1 wins the bugs round because "its bugs are cosmetic. V2's bugs have consequences." V1 scores 7/10 vs V2's 5/10. Also Round 4: "V1 is readable — I'll give it that."

assertion_details.assertion: Includes a 'What the Loser Should Steal' section with actionable advice
assertion_details.evidence: "## What the Loser Should Steal" section with three numbered, actionable items: 1) Class-based design, 2) Max size + eviction, 3) Hit/miss tracking — each with specific guidance.

assertion_details.assertion: Roast lines use Snoop Dogg voice — slang, laid-back delivery, hip-hop references
assertion_details.evidence: Consistent use of Snoop-style language throughout: 'nephew', 'cuz', 'homie', 'loc', 'fo' real', 'you know what I'm sayin'', 'stash', 'Like a Snoop Dogg concert with no security at the door', 'not the good kind of smoke', 'script kiddie...like it's Wheel of Fortune'. Laid-back cadence with hip-hop references and slang are present in every finding.

assertion_details.assertion: Does NOT use chef/kitchen metaphors (that's the default personality)
assertion_details.evidence: No chef, kitchen, cooking, recipe, ingredient, or food-preparation metaphors found anywhere in the output. Metaphors used are street/neighborhood-themed (house keys on porch, velvet rope, glass jar, screen door, concert security).

assertion_details.assertion: Still identifies the hardcoded JWT secret as a CRITICAL issue
assertion_details.evidence: First finding: '🔓 SECURITY — Hardcoded JWT Secret' rated 'Severity: CRITICAL' with specific line reference (Line 5) and the exact offending code quoted.

assertion_details.assertion: Still identifies the admin escalation via query parameter
assertion_details.evidence: Second finding: '🔓 SECURITY — Admin Privilege Escalation via Query Param' — 'Lines 37-39 — isAdmin checks req.query.admin === "true" and mutates req.user.role to "admin".' Rated CRITICAL.

assertion_details.assertion: Still provides concrete fixes for each finding
assertion_details.evidence: All 9 findings include a **Fix:** section with concrete, copy-pasteable code blocks. Examples: environment variable for JWT secret, role check for admin middleware, expiresIn for JWT, HttpOnly cookie, input validation, Bearer token parsing, SQLite database, existence check, rate limiting.

assertion_details.assertion: Technical severity ratings are accurate (not inflated or deflated for comedy)
assertion_details.evidence: Hardcoded JWT secret: CRITICAL (correct — full token forgery). Admin escalation: CRITICAL (correct — complete authz bypass). No expiration: HIGH (correct — token reuse risk). localStorage: HIGH (correct — XSS token theft). No input validation: HIGH (correct — silent overwrite + no constraints). Bearer parsing: MEDIUM (correct — functional bug but lower impact). In-memory store: HIGH (correct — data loss on restart). No existence check: MEDIUM (correct — minor robustness issue). No rate limiting: MEDIUM (correct — brute force enabler but requires effort). Ratings are technically sound and not distorted by humor.

assertion_details.assertion: Output follows the standard format (Verdict, Findings, Scoreboard, Final Words)
assertion_details.evidence: Output contains all required sections in order: '## The Verdict' (with score), '## Findings' (9 individual findings with severity and fixes), '## The Scoreboard' (table with categories, issue counts, worst severity), '## Final Words' (closing commentary). Also includes the Auto-Fix prompt at the end.

assertion_details.assertion: The personality is consistent throughout — not just the intro
assertion_details.evidence: Snoop voice is maintained from Verdict ('Ayo...nephew') through every single finding quote ('Nephew', 'Cuz', 'homie', 'loc', 'baby'), into Final Words ('Aight look, I'ma keep it real with you, nephew...like a Snoop Dogg concert'). No section reverts to neutral/default tone. Even technical explanations maintain the voice ('goes *poof* like smoke. And not the good kind of smoke').

assertion_details.evidence: The output is a mode selection menu, not a completed roast. There is no Auto-Fix offer section anywhere in the output.

assertion_details.assertion: Auto-Fix section mentions the number of fixable issues found
assertion_details.evidence: No Auto-Fix section exists in the output. The output only contains mode and personality selection prompts.

assertion_details.assertion: Auto-Fix section includes the options: All, Critical/High only, Pick, Nah
assertion_details.evidence: No Auto-Fix section exists. The only options shown are mode selections (Standard Roast, Panel Roast, Eval Mode, Diff Roast, Batter Battle) and personality selections.

assertion_details.assertion: The Auto-Fix offer appears AFTER the Final Words section, not before
assertion_details.evidence: There is no Final Words section and no Auto-Fix offer in the output. The output is a pre-roast menu, not a completed review.

assertion_details.assertion: The roast itself is complete and valid before the Auto-Fix offer
assertion_details.evidence: No roast has been performed. The output is the initial mode selection prompt asking 'Which mode? (and personality if you want one)' — no code was reviewed.

assertion_details.assertion: The wrench emoji (🔧) appears in the Auto-Fix section
assertion_details.evidence: The wrench emoji 🔧 does not appear anywhere in the output. Emojis present are: 🍰, 🔥, 👨‍🍳, 📊, 📝, 🆚, 🧑‍🍳, 👵, 😐, 🎤, 🐕, 🏴‍☠️.
